coredata.dc:title: Biophysical and electrochemical properties of Self-assembled noncovalent SWNT/DNA hybrid and electroactive nanostructure
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Mirzapoor, Aboulfazl
Ranjbar, Bijan
coredata.dc:description: DNA self-assembled hybrid nanostructures are widely used in recent research in nanobiotechnology. Combination of DNA with carbon based nanoparticles such as single-walled carbon nanotube (SWNT), multi-walled carbon nanotube (MWNT) and carbon quantum dot were applied in important biological applications. Many examples of biosensors, nanowires and nanoelectronic devices, nanomachine and drug delivery systems are fabricated by these hybrid nanostructures. In this study, a new hybrid nanostructure has been fabricated by noncovalent interactions between single or double stranded DNA and SWNT nanoparticles and biophysical properties of these structures were studied comparatively. Biophysical properties of hybrid nanostructures studied by circular dichroism, UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques. Also, electrochemical properties studied by cyclic voltammetry, linear sweep voltammetry, square wave voltammetry, choronoamperometry and impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Results revealed that the biophysical and electrochemical properties of SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructures were different compare to ss-DNA, ds-DNA and SWNT singly. Circular dichroism results showed that ss-DNA wrapped around the nanotubes through π-π stacking interactions. The results indicated that after adding SWNT to ss-DNA and ds-DNA intensity of CD and UV–vis spectrum peaks were decreased. Electrochemical experiments indicated that the modification of single-walled carbon nanotubes by ss-DNA improves the electron transfer rate of hybrid nanostructures. It was demonstrated SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructures should be a good electroactive nanostructure that can be used for electrochemical detection or sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructures
Spectroscopic study
Circular dichroism
Electrochemical study

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of noncovalent SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructures
Immobilization of bioconjugate on surface of electrode
spectroscopy study of SWNT and SWNT/DNA
Circular dichroism studies of SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructures
Electrochemical properties of hierarchical, self-assembled SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructures

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption spectra of SWNT before and after 8h reflux in a 3:1V/V solution of concentrated sulfuric acid (98%) and was concentrated by nitric acid (70%).

UV–vis absorption spectra of (a) ss-DNA (prob2) and SWNT/ss-DNA noncovalent hybrid nanostructures in different concentrations of SWNT dispersed by SDS and PVP; (b) ss-DNA (prob2) and SWNT/ss-DNA noncovalent hybrid nanostructures in different concentrations of SWNT dispersed by PEG/Triton X-100;.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of ds-DNA and SWNT/ds-DNA noncovalent hybrid nanostructures in different concentrations of SWNT. (b) Fluorescence intensity of ds-DNA and SWNT/ds-DNA noncovalent hybrid nanostructures at different concentrations of SWNT dispersed by PEG/Triton X-100;.
CD spectra of ss-DNA with (a) 50µM and (b) 30µM concentration that interacted with different concentrations of SWNT dispersed by SDS/PVP.
CD spectra of ss-DNA with (a) 50µM and (b) 30µM concentration that interacted with different concentrations of SWNT dispersed by PEG/Triton X-100.

CD spectra of 50µM ds-DNA and (a) ds-DNA combined with different concentrations of SWNT dispersed by SDS/ PVP (b) ds-DNA interacted with different concentrations of SWNT that dispersed by PEG/Triton X-100.
(a) linear sweep voltammograms (b) cyclic voltammograms of GCE/SWNT, GCE/SWNT-ss-DNA in 10mM Fe (CN)6 3−/4− and 0.1M KCl solution at a potential sweep rate of 50mVs−1.
(a) choronoamperograms (b) Dependence of I on t1/2 (c) Square‐wave voltammograms of GCE/SWNT, GCE/SWNT-ss DNA in 10mM Fe (CN)6 3−/4− and 0.1M KCl solution.
(a) Nyquist diagrams of GCE/SWNT (), GCE/SWNT-ss DNA () electrode in 10mM Fe (CN)6 3−/4− and 0.1M KCl, DC potential is 350mV/Ag–AgCl (b) Equivalent circuits compatible with the Nyquist diagrams.
Schematic representation of the Self-assembled noncovalent SWNT/DNA hybrid and electroactive nanostructure.
Equivalent circuit parameters of SWNT, SWNT-Prob 2 at oxidation peak potentials from Fig. 9.

head.ce:title: Biophysical and electrochemical properties of Self-assembled noncovalent SWNT/DNA hybrid and electroactive nanostructure

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanobiotechnology, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, P.O. Box 14115-175, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Nanobiotechnology, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biophysics, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, P.O. Box 14115-175, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biophysics, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Nanobiotechnology, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, P.O. Box 14115-175, Tehran, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: DNA self-assembled hybrid nanostructures are widely used in recent research in nanobiotechnology. Combination of DNA with carbon based nanoparticles such as single-walled carbon nanotube (SWNT), multi-walled carbon nanotube (MWNT) and carbon quantum dot were applied in important biological applications. Many examples of biosensors, nanowires and nanoelectronic devices, nanomachine and drug delivery systems are fabricated by these hybrid nanostructures. In this study, a new hybrid nanostructure has been fabricated by noncovalent interactions between single or double stranded DNA and SWNT nanoparticles and biophysical properties of these structures were studied comparatively. Biophysical properties of hybrid nanostructures studied by circular dichroism, UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques. Also, electrochemical properties studied by cyclic voltammetry, linear sweep voltammetry, square wave voltammetry, choronoamperometry and impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Results revealed that the biophysical and electrochemical properties of SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructures were different compare to ss-DNA, ds-DNA and SWNT singly. Circular dichroism results showed that ss-DNA wrapped around the nanotubes through π-π stacking interactions. The results indicated that after adding SWNT to ss-DNA and ds-DNA intensity of CD and UV–vis spectrum peaks were decreased. Electrochemical experiments indicated that the modification of single-walled carbon nanotubes by ss-DNA improves the electron transfer rate of hybrid nanostructures. It was demonstrated SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructures should be a good electroactive nanostructure that can be used for electrochemical detection or sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: Single-walled carbon nanotube (SWNT)/DNA hybrid nanostructures used in various areas of scientific research, such as designing nanobiosensor, separation and purification of nanoparticles, nanobioelectronics, as well as fabrication of new nanodevices with novel functions and applications [1–5]. SWNT with short length is preferentially used for drug and gene delivery applications, while its long length is typically used in electronic applications [6–10]. Exceptional electrical properties of carbon nanotubes have led to the fastest growing use of this nanomaterial as ‘building blocks’ in nano/microelectronic devices and microchips. Specifically, SWNT has been utilized in fabrication of nanowire, nanocircuit and field effect transistors [1,11,12]. Importantly, SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructures have potential to be used in applicable nanodevices needless of chemical functionalisation. Pure SWNT has attractive physical properties such as high tensile strength, electrical and thermal conductivity however it does not exhibit circular dichroism signal due to having chiral structure. Meanwhile, covalent or noncovalent interactions between DNA or RNA and SWNT, lead to development of CD signals [13–17]. Various length of DNA biomolecule can be applied for solubilisation of SWNT as surfactant with covalent/noncovalent conjugation. UV–vis absorption spectroscopy is a useful analytical tool to monitor key features of SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructures [18–22]. However circular dichroism spectropolarimeter is considered as a better complementary technique for a careful structure study on DNA structure before and after conjugation with SWNT. Wrapping DNA around individual nanotube and strong π-π stacking interactions between aromatic DNA bases, as well as SWNT effect on plasmon resonances in the free electron cloud of the nanotube π electrons could be feasibly monitored via UV–vis spectroscopy [23]. Previous studies on SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructures have addressed the effect of DNA on physical and electrochemical SWNT properties [16–24]. Wang et al. illustrated that SWNT were dispersed by wrapping ss-DNA around of SWNT. The ss-DNA/SWNT showed good electrochemical voltammetric properties [25]. Additionally, functionalisation of SWNT with ss-DNA probes could also be used for direct and selective electrochemical detection of DNA targets [26]. However, the effect of SWNT on conformational changes of ss-DNA has not been studied sufficiently. In this regard, we report synthesis and characterization of SWNT/ss-DNA and SWNT/ds-DNA noncovalent hybrid nanostructures via circular dichroism spectropolarimetery. Also, electrochemical properties of these nanostructures were studied with different electrochemical techniques. This research is the first observation of anisotropic hypochromicity in SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructures by circular dichroism spectroscopy. In this case, optical transitions of bases of DNA which hybridized with SWNT studied by optical spectroscopy observation. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study which investigates the effects of SWNT on DNA and shows the hypochromic effects in CD spectrum of DNA.

SWNT (diameter =1.1–2nm, purity > 95%) was purchased from Neutrino company. Analytical grade 2-(N-morpholino) ethane sulfonic acid (MES) and Poly ethylene glycol (PEG) with average 10,000 molecular weight, were procured from Santa Cruz Biotechnology product. Triton X-100, sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) and other chemicals for buffer preparation were purchased from Merck. Potassium Ferro cyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6]) and Potassium Ferri cyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]) were purchased from Sigma product. Doubly distilled water was used in all experiments. DNA oligomers and Amino-end-functionalized DNA samples of HPLC grade were procured from Bioneer (South Korea) which are listed in Table 1.

SWNT was purified through refluxing SWNT in a 3:1V/V solution of concentrated sulfuric acid (98%) and was concentrated by nitric acid (70%) for 8h at 50°C. Acidic solution removed from SWNT by washing with water for six times until stabilizes pH on 6–7. The process led to functionalization of sidewall and open end of SWNT with carboxyl group [27–31].
I) Dispersion of SWNT by Triton X-100 and PEG.

0.5mg of shortened SWNT was suspended in 10ml of 0.5% Triton X-100 (V/V) and 0.5% (W/V) of PEG, sonicated (probe sonication, Up 450, Hielcher) for 10min at 80kHz. The solution was centrifuged at 16,000rpm for 30min. The supernatant consisting of dispersed and functionalized SWNT was collected and used for the next step [19].
II) Dispersion of SWNT by SDS and PVP.

0.5mg shortened SWNT was suspended in 10ml of 0.5% SDS (W/V) and 0.5% PEG (W/V), sonicated for 10min at a nominal power of 80kHz. The solution was centrifuged for 30min (23°C, 16,000rpm). The supernatant was removed for collection of functionalized SWNT.

Double-stranded sequence was prepared in hybridization buffer (100mM MES, pH 6.0) and by hybridization of complementary ss-DNA consisting of probe1, probe1′ and probe2 and using Thermoblock. To initiate formation of double-stranded DNA structure, mixture of three probe was heated to 80°C (10min) and gradually cooled down to room temperature [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of noncovalent SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructures
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, dispersed and functionalized SWNT was added to 100µL of 50µM Probe2 in MES buffer at a various SWNT/DNA mass ratios. After adding SWNT and mixing it with ss-DNA and shaking by fast shaker for 30min, ss-DNA was wrapped around SWNTs. SWNT/ds-DNA hybrid nanostructures prepared through interaction of SWNT to ds-DNA like this method. A straightforward method is described to fabrication of self-assembled SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructure as illustrated in Scheme 1. For characterization of hybrid nanostructures, UV–Vis absorbance spectra were recorded on a Cary 100 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. All spectra were measured in 1.0cm path-length cell with different concentrations of SWNT aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobilization of bioconjugate on surface of electrode
ce:section.ce:para: For immobilization of the hierarchical self-assembled SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructures and other modification layer on surface of electrode, first glassy carbon electrodes (GCE) were carefully polished with 1, 0.5 and 0.3µm α-alumina slurry successively, then washed with deionized water. The self-assembled bioconjugation solution (5µL), which contained noncovalent self-assembled SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructures were sonicated for 15min to achieve homogeneous dispersion. This suspension (5µL) was drop-cast on to the GCE surface and dried for one day at 4°C.

In order to study the effect of dispersed SWNT on ds-DNA, fluorescence assay was conducted on a LS 55 spectrofluorimeter (Perkin Elmer, Langen). Since the inherent fluorescence of DNA molecule is weak, hybridization of the complementary DNA strands was monitored using Pico Green [30,31]. Pico Green is a specific dye for double-stranded DNA (ds-DNA), which increase the fluorescence intensity to 1000 folds upon hybridization of two strands [33,34].

Circular dichroism spectropolarimeter is one of the best techniques to monitor conformational changes that might be induced in the structure of macromolecules [35–37]. CD spectra were recorded on a JASCO J-715 Spectropolarimeter (Japan) in the Far-UV region. The instrument was calibrated with (+)−10-camphorsulfonic acid, assuming [ɵ]291 = 7820degcm2 dmol−1, and with JASCO standard non-hydroscopic ammonium (+)−10- camphorsulfonate, assuming [ɵ]290.5 = 7910degcm2 dmol−1. The optical chamber of CD spectropolarimeter was deoxygenated with nitrogen for 10min at a speed of 5l/min before being used, and keeping the nitrogen atmosphere during the experiments. Data were smoothed using the JASCO J-715 software, including the fast Fourier transform noise reduction routine, which allows enhancement of most noisy spectra without distorting their peak shapes.

Electrochemical studies were carried out in a conventional three electrode cell powered by Ivium Stat. XR electrochemical analyzer (Eindhoven, Nether-lands). Impedance measurement was carried out with an Autolab potentiostat–galvanostat. A three-electrode cell with a glassy carbon (GC) working electrode, having 0.07cm2 surface area, and platinum wire auxiliary and Ag/AgCl (3M KCl) reference electrode. The GC disk electrode was polished and rinsed thoroughly with doubly distilled water prior to modification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: spectroscopy study of SWNT and SWNT/DNA
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows UV–vis spectra of intact SWNT and SWNT after 8h reflux in a 3:1V/V solution of concentrated sulfuric acid (98%) and was concentrated by nitric acid (70%) at 50°C and after washing with water for six times. According to this spectra, because of inter band transitions and plasmon resonance of P electrons, two absorption peaks were appeared in 230nm and 270nm. Murakami et al. have shown that there are two SWNT absorption peaks (centered at ~275 and ~236nm) in the UV–vis spectra of graphite structures [38]. Furthermore, wide and small peaks at 230–330nm in free SWNT solution absorption spectrum corresponds to plasmon resonances of free electron cloud of the nanotube p electrons [39]. This result shows SWNT after reflux maintained their structures and physical properties. Also, due to increasing carboxyl group of SWNT after reflux and more dispersion, absorption peak increased.

Fig. 2a & b show absorption spectra of ss-DNA that were combined to various concentrations of dispersed SWNT by SDS/PVP and SWNT dispersed by Triton X-100/PEG in the wavelength range of 220–340nm consequently. Reference cell contained 100µL of the buffer solution in MES to account for any absorption effects due to the buffer and the sample cell contained 100µL of 5µM ss-DNA in MES. The sharp peak around 270nm in the absorption spectrum of free ss-DNA corresponds to the electronic transitions of DNA bases. Following this, in each stage 10µL of 0.1mg/ml SWNT dispersed solution was added to the sample and reference cell. Due to interaction between π-π stacking planes of the aromatic nucleotide bases of ss-DNA and surface of the nanotube, ss-DNA wrapped around SWNT [40]. Because of in-plane electron transitions in the bases of DNA, after interaction of ss-DNA with SWNT a hypochromicity is expected to appear in UV absorption of hybrid nanostructures. We can see this hypochromicity effect in two groups of SWNT/ss-DNA hybrid nanostructures.

Fig. 3a depicts the absorption spectra of ds-DNA and ds-DNA interacted to various concentrations of SWNT (0.1mg/ml to 1mg/ml) dispersed by PEG/Triton X- 100. This result shows hypochromicity effect appeared in SWNT/ds-DNA hybrid nanostructures but the effect of SWNT on ds-DNA is different compare to ss-DNA. At the low concentrations of SWNT, intensity of the absorption peak increased at 260nm. This effect could be result of local transition of ds-DNA to ss-DNA. However, as the concentration of SWNT increases, wrapping of DNA around of SWNT, leading to decrease of absorption intensity at 260nm. Fig. 3b depicts Pico Green (Pico Green is a specific dye for double-stranded DNA) fluorescence alterations for ds-DNA at various concentrations of SWNT (0.1mg/ml to 1mg/ml). After addition of 0.1mg/ml SWNT to ds-DNA, fluorescence intensity of Pico green decreased suddenly. However, upon increment of SWNT concentration, the fluorescence intensity slightly increased. There could be two reasons for changes in Pico Green fluorescence intensity. Really, in low concentration of SWNT, interaction between SWNT and ds-DNA caused local transition of ds-DNA to ss-DNA, leading to decrease of dye intercalation; whereas at higher concentration of SWNT and in crowded condition, ds-DNA wrapped around SWNT and maintained these secondary structure, so that higher amounts of Pico green connected to SWNT/ds-DNA hybrid nanostructures and Pico Green fluorescence intensity increased with increasing of SWNT concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Circular dichroism studies of SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructures
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4a and b show circular dichroism spectra of ss-DNA (50µM and 30µM) and ss-DNA that interacted with different concentrations of SWNT dispersed with SDS and PVP. CD spectrum of ss-DNA revealed two CD signals with opposite signs around 247nm and 280nm. Although B-DNA has a conservative CD spectrum above 220nm with approximately equal positive 275nm and negative 245nm components centered around 260nm [41,42], conformation of this symmetric ss-DNA “probe2″ in MES Buffer could be considered as B-DNA as well. On the other hand, since this single strand DNA is thymine rich, positive and negative peak in CD spectrum partially shifts to higher wavelength. There are a few reports on possibility of studying interaction between DNA or RNA & carbon nanotubes claimed that interaction between carbon nanotubes and DNA or RNA cause to disturb the nucleic acid hydration layer and change DNA structures could be studied by circular dichroism spectroscopy [15,16]. However, none of them have addressed the obvious impact of SWNT on CD spectrum of ss-DNA and SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructures. In this study, SWNT was added to ss-DNA step by step, until 1:1 SWNT/DNA ratio was obtained. According to literature, at (1:1) ratio of DNA and nanoparticle (NP), the digested and ligated DNA templates are able to maintain B-form DNA structure with both metallic and magnetic NPs [43,44].

CD spectrum of SWNT/ss-DNA showed that ss-DNA has maintained its B-form conformation and peak positions of both CD signals conserved but their intensity changed. Below 300nm, the CD spectra showed intense CD signals associated with electronic transitions of both nanotubes and DNA bases [15,45]. It should be noted that after addition of SWNT to ss-DNA, oligonucleotides wrapped around of SWNT that caused to both positive and negative CD signals decreased. However, in this study SWNT reduced CD intensity of both DNA. CD spectra of ss-DNA shows reduction of signal intensity for both positive and negative peaks, around 280nm and 250nm respectively. Such changes could be due to coupling DNA and SWNT and exactly wrapping of DNA around SWNT. Indeed, the spectra revealed the characteristic hypochromicity circular dichroism (HCD) of noncovalent SWNT/ss-DNA hybrid nanostructures in different concentrations of dispersed SWNT. A clear change in the spectral intensity has been observed, and could be correlated with SWNT/biomolecule interaction.

Fig. 5a and b show circular dichroism spectra of ss-DNA with 50µM and 30µM concentration before and after interaction with SWNT dispersed by Triton X-100 and PEG. Similar to previous steps, dispersed SWNT added to ss-DNA step by step, until 1:1 SWNT/DNA ratio was obtained. Due to more reduction in positive and negative peaks and also blue shift in positive signal in CD spectra revealed, effect of dispersed SWNT on ss-DNA was stronger than that of SWNT dispersed by SDS and PVP. The hypochromicity effects could be monitored by circular dichroism of SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructures. After addition of SWNT to ss-DNA, both CD signals are conserved with changes in intensity. However, the rate of change of two signals is not equal, namely the positive signal change rate is faster than the negative one.

Fig. 6a and b show circular dichroism spectra of ds-DNA (50µM) before and after interaction with SWNT dispersed by SDS and PVP and with SWNT dispersed by Triton X-100/PEG, respectively. As it was mentioned, ds-DNA was prepared by hybridization of complementary ss-DNA. CD spectra of ds-DNA show two negative and positive signals at 250nm and 280nm. Upon interaction with SWNT, CD spectra of ds-DNA undergoes decrease in intensity of negative and positive peaks due to hypochromicity effect, with little shift in negative signal from 250nm to 255nm, but the positive signal is constant. In low concentration of SWNT positive and negative CD signals decreased equally that could be considered ds-DNA changed to ss-DNA. Whereas, SWNT concentration increased, DNA wrapped around the SWNT symmetrically. It should be noted that decrease of signal intensity of SWNT/ds-DNA hybrid nanostructures CD is more than of SWNT/ss-DNA hybrid nanostructures, because of SWNT stacking with ds-DNA is more symmetric than ss-DNA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties of hierarchical, self-assembled SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructures
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical properties of all self-assembled SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructures were studied by cyclic voltammetry, linear sweep voltammetry, square wave voltammetry choronoamperometry and impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in solutions containing 10mM Fe (CN)6 3−/4− and 0.1M KCl. Fig. 7a shows linear sweep voltammograms of GCE/SWNT and GCE/SWNT/ss-DNA self-assembled hybrid nanostructures electrode at a potential sweep rate of 50mVs−1. From this figure, we can see the oxidation peak currents increased by noncovalent interaction of SWNT and ss-DNA.

The CV analysis of the redox reaction of Fe (CN)6 3− and Fe (CN)6 4− is a well-known method to characterize electrochemical behavior of modified and bare electrodes and the simplest way to determine electron transfer rate [46]. The CV responses of all modified electrodes displayed a classical sigmoidal shape with different peak-to-peak potential separations. The larger peak separation indicated slow electron transfer kinetics [47]. Cyclic voltammograms of GCE/SWNT, GCE/SWNT/ss-DNA hybrid nanostructures electrode in 10mM Fe (CN)6 3−/4− and 0.1M KCl solution at a potential sweep rate of 50mVs−1 illustrated in Fig. 7b. As can be seen in all electrodes narrow peak-to-peak separations, indicating that fast electron transfer. But anodic and cathodic peaks current of GCE/SWNT/ss-DNA is more than GCE/SWNT. Obviously, π-orbital overlap between the stacked base pairs along the axis of DNA could provide a one dimensional pathway for migration of electrical charge [48]. Due to the wrapping ss-DNA around SWNT and significant π–π intermolecular electron transfer between DNA and SWNT and also weak oxidation of base in nucleotide fast electron transfer appeared in SWNT/ss-DNA. In fact, because of electrostatic repulsion of the charges in hybrid nanostructures, these structures were facilitate transferred electron quickly in solution. According to LSV and CV results of modified electrodes the peak current of Fe (CN)6 3−/4− in GCE/SWNT/ss-DNA increased compare to electrode modified only with SWNT, indicating that the interaction of ss-DNA with SWNT played deceive role in creating conductive and electroactive nanostructures.

Fig. 8a shows choronoamperograms for GCE/SWNT, GCE/SWNT/ss-DNA hybrid nanostructures electrode in 10mM Fe (CN)6 3−/4− and 0.1M KCl solution at oxidation peak potential. Plotting of the net current with respect to the inverse of the square roots of time presents a linear dependency for two modified electrodes (Fig. 8b). The dominance of a diffusion controlled process is evident [49].

Similarly, Square wave Voltammograms of GCE/SWNT, GCE/SWNT/ss-DNA hybrid nanostructures electrode in 10mM Fe (CN)6 3−/4− and 0.1 KCl solution were obtained with f = 2Hz, ΔES = 10mV and potential sweep rate of 50mVs−1 illustrate in Fig. 8c. Moreover, the magnitude of the SWV peak signal of SWNT/ss-DNA modified electrode enhancement and increased relative to the GCE/SWNT electrode. The enhancement in the peak current is due to the electroactivity of hybrid nanostructures.

The electron transfer properties of different electrodes were further characterized by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The EIS analyzer software was chosen to fit the impedance out puts. Fig. 9a shows the Nyquist diagrams of GCE/SWNT, GCE/ SWNT/ss-DNA hybrid nanostructures electrode in potential of oxidation peak. (Potential as dc) The Nyquist diagrams contain semicircular in low frequency that can be related to RCT and linear portions in the high frequency sides of the spectrum. The equivalent circuit compatible with the Nyquist diagram is presented in Fig. 9b. In this electrical equivalent circuit, Rs, RCT and W represent solution resistance, charge transfer resistance and the Warburg that is electrical elements related to the diffusion processes. Qdl is constant phase element (CPE), corresponding to the double layer capacitance [50]. From the plots and fitting process, the values of RCT were obtained, as shown in Table 2. The value of RCT of GCE/SWNT, GCE/SWNT/ss-DNA hybrid nanostructures electrode (3204.8Ω & 1771.7Ω) indicated that the electron transfer resistance of SWNT/ss-DNA modification layer reduced relative to electrode modified only with SWNT. In contrast, modification of SWNT by ss-DNA improved electron transfer rate and conductivity in GCE/SWNT/ss-DNA hybrid nanostructures electrode.

In this study, the effect of SWNT on ss-DNA and ds-DNA has been investigated. We showed that ss-DNA and ds-DNA interacted with SWNT by noncovalent interaction. UV–vis and CD spectra of SWNT/DNA hybrid nanostructures showed intensity of peaks was decreased that could be illustrated two type of DNA wrapped around of SWNT. However, what is clear in our study is that hypochromicity effect confirms the π-π stacking of ss-DNA and ds-DNA bases with SWNT. Noncovalent interaction between DNA and SWNT, generate hypochromicity circular dichroism (HCD). Also, it was found that interaction of ss-DNA with dispersed SWNT is different compare to ds-DNA with SWNT. Upon interaction with SWNT, CD signals of ds-DNA are symmetric; whereas they become asymmetric in case of ss-DNA. Electrochemical experiments indicated that the modification of single-walled carbon nanotubes by ss-DNA improves the electron transfer rate GCE/SWNT/ss-DNA hybrid nanostructures electrode, which is verified by AC impedance spectra. Electrochemical results confirmed ss-DNA wrapped around SWNT. These findings encourage the possibility of synthesizing a new series of nanostructures via covalent and noncovalent conjugation of DNA with nanoparticles of gold, silver, alloy nanoparticles and nanoclusters, with high levels of application diversity in nanotechnology.

Bijan Ranjbar planned experiments, conceived the study and analyzed the data. Aboulfazl Mirzapoor performed and analyzed the circular dichroism, UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy studies. All authors contributed to writing and preparation of the manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the research council of Tarbiat Modares University for Financial support of this work.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Handbook of Nanostructured Biomaterials and Their Applications in Nano biotechnology

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: V.A.. Bloomfield, D.M.. Crothers, I.. Jr Tinoco, with contribution from, J.E. Hearst, D.E. Wemmer, P.A. Kollman, D.H. Turner, Nucleic Acids: Structures, Properties, and Functions. Sausalito, California, USA: University Science Books, 2000.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Charge Migration in DNA Perspectives from Physics, Chemistry, and Biology

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: A.J. Bard, L.R. Faulkner, A.J. Bard (Ed.) Electrochemical Methods, Fundamentals and Applications, Wiley, New York, 2001, p. 209.
